content.text: Do 4 (four) of the first 5 (1–5) and 4 (four) of the last 5 problems (6–10).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text:
Determine constants \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) such that the following inequality holds for the \(p\) and \(\infty\) norms of matrix \(A\), for integers \(p\geq 1\). Justify your answer.
\[
\alpha\|A\|_\infty\leq \|A\|_p\leq \beta\|A\|_\infty.
\]

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove or give a counterexample: \(\|A\|_2\leq \|A\|_F\), where \(\|A\|_F\) is the Frobenius norm of \(A\). If you prove this, make sure to justify each nontrivial step.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Prove that each principal submatrix of \(A\) is Hermitian positive definite.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Prove that an element of \(A\) with largest magnitude lies on the diagonal.

subparts.label: (c)
subparts.text: Prove that \(A\) has a Cholesky decomposition.

problem.text:
Define the matrices \(A\) and \(B\) by
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}
1&2&0\\
1&2&0\\
1&2&0\\
1&2&0
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B=\begin{pmatrix}
1&0&1\\
0&2&0\\
1&0&0\\
0&0&1
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Find both full and economy singular value decompositions of \(A\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Find both full and economy QR decompositions of \(B\).

problem.text: Let \(\|\cdot\|\) be a subordinate (induced) matrix norm.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text:
If \(E\) is \(n\times n\) with \(\|E\|<1\), then show \(I+E\) is nonsingular and
\[
\|(I+E)^{-1}\|\leq \frac{1}{1-\|E\|}.
\]

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
If \(A\) is \(n\times n\) invertible and \(E\) is \(n\times n\) with \(\|A^{-1}\|\|E\|<1\), then show \(A+E\) is nonsingular and
\[
\|(A+E)^{-1}\|\leq \frac{\|A^{-1}\|}{1-\|A^{-1}\|\|E\|}.
\]

problem.text:
Suppose the linear equation \(Ax=b\), with
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}\delta&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix},\qquad |\delta|<\epsilon_m/4,
\]
is solved on a floating-point system with machine-epsilon \(\epsilon_m\), using an LU factorization (no pivoting!) followed by forward and back substitution. (You may assume the operations \((+,-,\div,\times)\), do not incur any additional errors.)

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: If \(b=(1,0)^T\), compute the backward error, \(\|\hat b-b\|_2/\|b\|_2\), where \(\hat b\) is the data that satisfies \(A\hat x=\hat b\), and \(\hat x\) is the computed solution.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Is the result of (a) sufficient to draw any conclusions about the backward-stability of the algorithm used to compute \(\hat x\)? Explain.

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Starting with the first two Legendre polynomials over \([-1,1]\), given by \(p_0(x)=1\), and \(p_1(x)=x\), find the next three (monic) Legendre polynomials, \(p_2(x),p_3(x)\) and \(p_4(x)\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
Find the nodes \(t_0,t_1,t_2\) and weights \(w_0,w_1,w_2\) which define the Gaussian Quadrature formula
\[
\int_{-1}^1 f(t)\,dt\approx w_0f(t_0)+w_1f(t_1)+w_2f(t_2),
\]
with degree of exactness \(m=5\). You should find the nodes exactly, and may leave the weights \(w_0,w_1,w_2\), in integral form.

problem.text: Consider the fixed point problem \(x=f(x)\), where \(f(x)=e^{-(3+x)}\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Assuming all computations are done in exact arithmetic, find the largest open interval in \(\mathbb{R}\) where the fixed-point iteration \(x_{k+1}=f(x_k)\) is ensured to converge.

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Write a Newton iteration for finding the fixed-point.

problem.text: Let \(P_1\) be the space of polynomials of degree at most one. Using the norm \(\|u\|_2=\left(\int_a^b u^2\,dx\right)^{1/2}\)

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Find the least-squares approximation to \(f(x)=x^3\) in \(P_1\) over \([a,b]=[-1,1]\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Find the least-squares approximation to \(f(x)=x^4\) in \(P_1\) over \([a,b]=[0,1]\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Find a natural cubic spline \(B_0\) on \(-1\leq x\leq 1\) that satisfies \(v(-1)=0\), \(v(0)=2\), and \(v(1)=0\).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text: Find a natural cubic spline on \(-1\leq x\leq 2\) that satisfies \(v(-1)=0\), \(v(0)=2\), \(v(1)=1/2\) and \(v(2)=0\). You may write the solution as \(B=B_0+B_1\), where \(B_0\) is (or is closely related to) the solution to (a), if you like.

problem.text: Let \(f\in C^\infty(a-H,a+H)\), and let \(h<H\). Let \(x_0=a-h\), \(x_1=a\) and \(x_2=a+h\).

subparts.label: (a)
subparts.text: Find the finite difference approximation to \(f''(a)\) based on the quadratic interpolant \(p_2\) which satisfies \(p_2(x_0)=f(x_0)\), \(p_2(x_1)=f(x_1)\) and \(p_2(x_2)=f(x_2)\) (you should explicitly show how the difference approximation is derived from the interpolant).

subparts.label: (b)
subparts.text:
Let \(\psi_0(h)=\psi(h)\) be the difference approximation to \(f''(a)\) found in part (a). Assume (in exact arithmetic) \(\psi(h)\to\psi(0)=f''(a)\) as \(h\to 0\), and that \(\psi(h)\) has the asymptotic expansion
\[
\psi(h)=\psi(0)+a_2h^2+a_4h^4+a_6h^6+\cdots.
\]
Find the general Richardson extrapolation formula for \(\psi_k(h)\) based on \(\psi_{k-1}(h)\) and \(\psi_{k-1}(h/2)\).
